reviews.review_text:
Totally Accurate Battle Simulator (or the acronym I will be using in this review TABS)  is probably the least serious game I have played in a good while. I had a reasonable amount of fun playing this and will try to outline the main strengths and weaknesses below. 

The Positives:

TABS is as the name suggests, a simulation of differing battles where you place different units to achieve this. It very similar to the popular auto chess genre seen in games like Dota Underlords and Teamfight Tactics. However what differs here is the currency you have to spend on each round is limited, alongside a limited amount units you can select as a result of this as well as what is available. This is a good moment to state the bulk of my playtime was spent playing the Campaign, so most of my review is going to be based on the content there. 

Getting back to the gameplay, the amount of units you have in the game is decent and a respectable number, where there are various moments you can mix and match different factions for interesting results. The game difficulty is not too easy as well, as using the same strategy can work, but is often dismantled by the next round, where a new formation of units is needed to overcome it. It also very amusing watching the fights play out as failing can be just as fun as seeing the enemy teams units fail at times which can lead to surprising wins. You are able to possess units, which allows you to fight as them if you prefer a more active role when playing the game. 

I found the campaigns in the game interesting as some felt very simple, but the last few had interesting rounds that I felt were very challenging. As most of my playtime came from this, and there a reasonable amount of content here for the price point of the game, I am mostly satisfied with my experience here. 

I think the maps also have some secrets, but I did not explore this too much and only found one with my playtime, but I am sure there a guide to find them all. Each secret found I believe gives you a hidden unit, but these units were not required for me to complete all the campaigns. It likely that these hidden units would make more difficult sections in campaigns more easier though, so you may wish to look into this if struggling (which you can in sandbox mode I think?). 

The presentation of the game, although simple, is effective as it gets you into the fights quickly and allows you to look at the various other modes it has to offer. I have tried out the multiplayer mode very briefly, but if you have a friend who also interested, I am sure you can have a great day messing around with the many units the game has to offer. I also have seen some comments saying the game should have ranked multiplayer, but I personally think it goes against it casual nature, and feel it would not add much to the game. 

The music is also fairly simple, but it works with the game given the minimal presentation, and helps to give a bit of whimsy to the fights due to the humorous nature of them. I also liked how certain units made unique noises which helped to give them an identity over other units and made them feel distinct, along with the differing attacks each unit has as well.

Lastly the game does support workshop content, which is always nice to see. I have not looked into this too much, but I am sure there a lot here to explore as well. 

The Negatives:

The game is as I said above, very simple. There not too much depth to the game other than placing down the units, getting a better idea how each unit works, and which formation to place them in. This is fine for the opening for the game, but I would of liked to see some more mechanics explored in the game. I will try to outline a  few of these ideas below.

I feel this game hints at the idea about wanting to explore fights in different terrains, but I never felt it was fully explored. Perhaps the developers wanted the players to build these maps and fully realise this idea, but I would of appreciated more maps that cared about the terrain. For instance there could of been levels that had lava pits, traps laid out, or trees in the middle of the battlefield. The game does try this idea on some maps, but there too few in my opinion. 

To extend my last point above, I suspect these maps with these terrain characteristics were not implemented was due to the random elements of how fights played out. I hinted it before with the fun element of units failing at times, but it can be frustrating to lose randomly due to something you could not factor in going wrong. It can lead to learning the units behaviours, but when getting this detailed on characteristics of the units, it can take the fun away from the game. Possessing units may also make the random elements of units success more bearable, but it not something I tried too much as I was treating the game more as a unit placement strategy game, and so did not take advantage of this active gameplay style. 

Another idea I would of liked to see explored is environmental factors, like ice physics, or terrain breaking apart due to weight of units. None of this is tried in the game as I assume it was not in scope for the game to implement this, but without this it makes combat feel very repetitive after a while. I feel if this and more maps with unique terrain was in the game, I would have enjoyed the game more as it would make me consider other units to compensate for these factors. 

There is some units I think are quite weak and have very limited uses, and unfortunately the inverse is true too as there a few units that became a bit too reliable at there role of being good. This made the game feel a bit figured out when seeing how effective they were. Again if there was more consideration on environmental factors this may of made the better units be outclassed by weaker units due to having to compensate for this. 

Lastly I highly recommend anyone who wishes to keep their sanity to not 100% the achievements for this game. They look like a nightmare to complete. 

Conclusion:

Totally Accurate Battle Simulator is a fun casual strategy game, which gives the various units their own charm and whimsy. The game is simple and repetitive, but provides interesting battle scenarios for you to try to solve with your own army. Thanks to it multiplayer mode and workshop integration, there should be lots of content here to enjoy. If this is not of interest to you, the campaign is reasonably long and provides a decent experience to what the game offers. I give this a 7/10.

Few games bring back memories quite like this one. I first played it back in its alpha days in 2017, when watching wobbly armies collide in ridiculous physics-driven battles felt like something completely fresh. Years later, finally finishing it was a nostalgic trip that reminded me why I loved it so much in the first place.

The low-poly art style remains one of my favorite aspects of the game. It's simple, colorful, and full of charm, perfectly matching the chaotic nature of every battle. Whether I was experimenting with absurd unit combinations or watching carefully planned strategies fall apart in seconds, the game constantly found new ways to make me laugh.

What stands out most is how well it embraces its own absurdity. It never tries to be serious, and that's exactly what makes it special. Between the creative unit designs, the sandbox freedom, and the endless hilarious moments, it delivers an experience that is unlike anything else.

A huge thank you to Landfall Games for creating such a memorable game. TABS has provided countless hours of fun over the years, and it's one of those rare games that never loses its charm no matter how many battles you've watched unfold.

This game is a ton of fun. There's a huge amount of variety in the units available and fact that you can play with modded maps and factions makes it even better.

TABS is one of the most entertaining games I’ve ever played. The physics‑based battles are absolutely ridiculous in the best way possible — every match feels like a brand‑new comedy sketch. Watching wobbly warriors charge into battle, fall over, or accidentally take out their own teammates never gets old.

The unit variety is awesome, the sandbox mode is endless fun, and the surprise moments are what make the game unforgettable. It’s the perfect mix of strategy and silliness, and even when you lose, you’re laughing.

If you love creativity, chaos, and games that don’t take themselves too seriously, TABS is a 5‑star experience all the way.
this game gets pretty boring after the levels are completed so i would recommend this game but not for everyone.

Totally accurate battle simulator is a wonderful game where two sides (#FF0000 and #0000FF) fight each other to the death with often goofy weapons, like the sythe, spear, and MBT! The game consists of a bunch of different worlds with a specific numer of levels per map. with each level you get a level specific amount of money to use on charecters and the goal is to win (obviously what else did you think?). 

TABS, however is so much more than just a campain based game where the final goal is to complete the game. You would also be able to play multiplayer against others (if you had friends that is). This creates goofy matches where you can simulate massive battles against eachother. Resulting in some very fond memories for those with friends.

another part of the game is charecter development. Here you can create different all sorts of creatures, like a dwarf with a minigun or jesus himself. you can even use these charecters in the campain as playables to save the battlefield from the evil devil and create a paradise for all living beings. Or maybe just a guy create a guy with a frying pan!

one part of the game that i especially like is when instead of spending your budget on something actually tactical, like a massive army or 1000 halflings. you instead create a military style formation that some people would call "more tactical" and "usefull". This, however, is something that Jesus doesnt approve of which may lead to him banishing all of us to the spirit realm to wander around as echos of and old past. luckily you can prevent this from happening by making a guy with wings and minigun flying around with stats reaching a googolplex. but this shouldn't be neccisary if you just play the game like the normal friendless player that you are. Im just telling you to be carefull while playing so you dont start another crusaid.

So if you are looking for a quick dose of adrenaline, a game against your √-1 friend, or just the need to start a holy war. TABS is the correct game for you!

A silly battle simulator game where you make a bunch of wobbly red dudes fight a bunch of wobbly blue dudes. There's a plethora of units to play with, sandbox and campaign modes (with many, many levels to play through), exaggerated physics, and in-depth tools to create your own units, battles, maps, campaigns, and factions. It even has multiplayer to battle your friends! All it all it has everything you'd want in a battle simulator game and then some, so if those are your sorta thing this is a must play that'll probably take all your free time for awhile. I recommend at market price.
This game is super duper fun i recommend it to anyone who loves strategy and fun chaos!
its a cool and fun game, with lots of achivements to chase and strategies to think of!
amazing game, and plenty of customization available! great game, i dont think it gets much updates, but the game is still great.
good game to play whit your freidns in mutiplayer or single player buy this game its is very good
Fun game overall, reccomend to play, the janky physics of the game makes the game really interesting every run
the only bad thing about this game is the units are BEYOND stupid and will in fact do the stupidest things and the long ranged units can shoot down or follow your mouse but its pretty good otherwise

This game is genuinly an amazing game it has a very simple premise, stupid looking guys fight each other. and yet is able to do so much with it the campaigns are fun and challenging and the core set of units are really fun 

not to mention the amount of content. i mean there abut 15ish different campaigns and a whole host of secret units hidden all over the map. Hell i havent even found them all yet!

and in my opinion this games absolute magnum opus is the character creator i have probably spent most of my time in this game in the creator there at least a few hundread different cosmetics that u can equip ur wobbler with (wobbler is the name of the funny guys you use to fight) and thats not even mentioning all the different weapons charateristics and abilities you can equip them with ive made about 20 units with the editior and could definetly make at least 50 more 

theres a lot more i could say but this review is long enough this game is amazing the only downside is that it no longer gets updates which is unfortunate but if u can find on sale or just want to buy it in full please by all means do.

I've always loved battles. There's very few games out there where you can build your own army and map and have them fight against each other! It's VERY accurate. I especially love the different mutations the units can get, from big heads, small, big, etc. The best part is placing a bunch of archers on one side and shields on the other, when the archers fire, slow down the gameplay and watch the shielders block or get shot. The physics is the best part of this game!

This game feels like riding a bike except you don't have any wheels, or a seat,
because the balancing in this game is terrible, that is why it's the greatest sandbox game I've ever played, the gameplay may get old after around the 200 hour mark. That is why there is the unit creator exists, for you to make your own gameplay, that may get boring after the 300 hour mark, but then I like to challenge myself, by completing all the campaigns with the Bard for example, or maybe halflings. Point is the game is the greatest game I've ever played.
Simple to understand and oddly enjoyable. Certain campaign maps requires exploiting the enemies' dumb AI, while others is based on luck. Otherwise it's a quite fun.
